revision.text:
[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': - Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge - Substructure: [[Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[Toei Bus]] - [[Ō49]]: [[Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] - Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] - [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[Kameari Station]] North Exit - Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] - [[Akabane 25]]: [[Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop - Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[Sumida River]]: - (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': - Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge - Substructure: [[Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[Toei Bus]] - [[Ō49]]: [[Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] - Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] - [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[Kameari Station]] North Exit - Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] - [[Akabane 25]]: [[Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop - Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[Sumida River]]: - (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': - Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge - Substructure: [[Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[Toei Bus]] - [[Ō49]]: [[Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] - Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] - [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[Kameari Station]] North Exit - Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] - [[Akabane 25]]: [[Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop - Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[Sumida River]]: - (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.

== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.

It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinkamiya Bridge]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Waterworks bridges in Japan]] [[Category:Bridges in Tokyo]] [[Category:Adachi City, Tokyo]] [[Category:Kita City, Tokyo]] [[Category:Bridges over the Sumida River]] [[Category:Roads opened in 1965]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Shinkamiya Bridge]] [[ja:新神谷橋]] [[sn:hinkamiya Bridge]] [[en:Shinkamiya Bridge]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Shinkamiya Bridge}}

[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

revision.text:
[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|東京都足立区から。(2008年7月)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=東京|number=318|align=left}} '''新神谷橋'''（しんかみやばし）は、[[東京都]][[足立区]]の[[隅田川]]にかかる[[都市計画道路]]環状七号[[東京都道318号環状七号線]]（'''環七通り'''）の[[橋]]（[[水道]]道路併用橋）である。
== 概要 == 橋長153.0メートル、3径間の鋼鈑[[桁橋]]の1等橋（TL-20<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|三上澄|佐藤次郎|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>）である。橋の右岸側には延長139.1メートルで幅員10.0メートル、左岸側は延長444.3メートルで幅員10.0メートルの取り付け道路を有している<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />。また、水道道路併用橋でもあり<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />橋に付帯設備として内径1.2メートルの水道管を4本（送水本管2本、工業用水道管2本）併設し、[[水路橋|水道橋]]の役割も持ちあわせている<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />。 また、災害時に防災拠点等に緊急輸送を行なうための、東京都の特定[[緊急輸送道路]]に指定されている<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|和書|date=2013 |url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/ |title=特定緊急輸送道路図 |publisher=東京都耐震ポータルサイト |accessdate=2017-03-30 |archiveurl= |archivedate= }}</ref>。 北岸は[[足立区]][[新田 (足立区)|新田]]（しんでん）2丁目、南岸は[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[神谷 (東京都北区)|神谷]]1丁目に位置する。橋名は北区の地名にちなむ。[[岩淵水門]]の管理橋を別とすれば、隅田川最上流の橋となる。
=== 諸元 === * 種別 - 鋼道路橋 * 形式 ** 上部工 - ゲルバー（カンチレバー）プレートガーダー/単純活荷重合成プレートガーダー橋 ** 下部工 - [[鉄筋コンクリート|RC]]躯体/鋼管杭基礎（外径600 mm、長さ14.0-16.5 m）<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * 橋長 - 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|和書|date=1965-03 |url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf |title=土木学会誌 第五十巻 第三号 |publisher=土木学会 |format=PDF |pages=87-88（pp. 4-5） |accessdate=2017-02-24 |archiveurl= |archivedate= }}</ref> * 総幅員 - 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|三上澄|佐藤次郎|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * 有効幅員 - 20.0 m（車道8.0 m、歩道2.0 m） * 径間割 - 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * 開通 - [[1965年]]（[[昭和]]40年）[[2月12日]]<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * 事業主体 - 東京都
== 歴史 == もともと「[[隅田川の渡し|宮堀の渡し]]（神谷の渡しとも）」があったところである。現在の位置より100メートルほど南の[[王子神谷駅]]付近の[[神谷堀]]（甚兵衛堀とも呼ばれる）に[[昭和]]2年（[[1927年]]）に木鉄混交の橋として架けられた「神谷橋」があり、のち昭和25年（[[1950年]]）に鋼[[桁橋]]として改架された。新しく隅田川に架けることとなった当橋はこの名称にちなむ。昭和52年（[[1977年]]）に神谷堀が埋め立てられ[[廃橋]]となったことから、現在は神谷橋は現存しない。ちなみに、「神谷橋」の名は[[東京メトロ南北線]][[王子神谷駅]]の仮称としても使われていた。
橋は首都圏整備整備の一環として第一期工事が[[1962年]]（昭和37年）10月着工された。[[1965年]]（[[昭和]]40年）の創架当初は仮開通状態で、片側1車線の幅員10.0メートルであったが、[[1967年]]（昭和42年）2月<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm 新神谷橋1965-2-12] - 土木学会付属土木図書館、2017年2月24日閲覧。</ref>にはすぐに追加の拡幅工事が行われ、現在の幅員となった。橋桁制作は[[松尾橋梁]]<ref name="doboku1965" />下部工は坂田建設が行なった<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />。
== 路線バス == * [[都営バス]] ** [[都営バス千住営業所#王49系統|王49]] [[王子駅]] - 北区神谷町 - 江北陸橋下- [[大師前駅|西新井大師]] - [[梅島駅]] - [[都営バス千住営業所|千住車庫]] ** 王49 王子駅 - 北区神谷町 - 江北陸橋下 - 江北駅(平日朝のみ) ** 王49折返 王子駅 - 北区神谷町 - 江北陸橋下 - 西新井大師 - [[足立区役所]] * [[東武バスセントラル]] ** [[東武バスセントラル葛飾営業所#亀有駅北口 - （環七通り） - 足立区役所・王子駅線|王30]] 王子駅 - 北区神谷町 - 江北陸橋下 - 西新井大師 - [[綾瀬警察署]] - [[亀有駅]]北口（日中2往復のみ） ** 深夜31 王子駅 → 北区神谷町 → 江北陸橋下 → 西新井大師 → [[西新井駅]](平日深夜のみ) * [[国際興業バス]] ** [[国際興業バス赤羽営業所#赤羽駅東口 - ハートアイランド循環線|赤25]] [[赤羽駅]]東口 - 東十条四丁目 - ハートアイランド循環 **赤25-2 赤羽駅東口 → 東十条四丁目 → ハートアイランド西 → ハートアイランド東(夜間のみ) **赤25-3 ハートアイランド西 → ハートアイランド東 → 東十条四丁目 → 赤羽駅東口(早朝のみ) ** [[国際興業バス赤羽営業所#廃止・移管路線|赤26]] 赤羽駅東口 - 東十条四丁目 - 加賀団地 - 舎人団地 ** [[国際興業バス赤羽営業所#赤羽駅東口 - 江北陸橋（環七経由）- 西新井駅線|赤27]] 赤羽駅東口 - 東十条四丁目- 江北陸橋下 - 西新井大師 - [[西新井駅]] ** 赤27-2 [[国際興業バス赤羽営業所|赤羽車庫]] - 東十条四丁目 - 江北陸橋下 - 西新井大師 - 西新井駅 ** 赤27-3 赤羽駅東口 → 東十条四丁目 → 江北陸橋下(土曜夜間のみ)
== 隣の橋 == * [[隅田川]] : （上流） - [[岩淵水門]] - '''新神谷橋''' - [[新田橋 (東京都)|新田橋]] - [[新豊橋]] - （下流）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite journal|和書|author=三上澄 |author2=佐藤次郎 |date=1965-07-01 |year=1965 |title=新神谷橋・鹿浜橋工事報告 |journal=[[土木技術 (雑誌)|土木技術]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=土木技術社 |issn=0285-5046 |ref=harv }}
== 関連項目 == * [[水道道路]] * [[鹿浜橋]] - 本橋のすぐ東側にある環状七号線の橋梁。本橋と同様に水道管を4本併設する。
{{隅田川の橋}} {{Coord|35|46|15|N|139|44|10|E|display=title|region:JP_type:landmark}} {{DEFAULTSORT:しんかみやはし}} [[Category:日本の水道橋]] [[Category:東京都区部の橋]] [[Category:足立区の橋]] [[Category:東京都北区の交通]] [[Category:隅田川の橋]] [[Category:1965年開業の道路施設]]

[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|東京都足立区から。(2008年7月)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=東京|number=318|align=left}} '''新神谷橋'''（しんかみやばし）は、[[東京都]][[足立区]]の[[隅田川]]にかかる[[都市計画道路]]環状七号[[東京都道318号環状七号線]]（'''環七通り'''）の[[橋]]（[[水道]]道路併用橋）である。
== 概要 == 橋長153.0メートル、3径間の鋼鈑[[桁橋]]の1等橋（TL-20<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|三上澄|佐藤次郎|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>）である。橋の右岸側には延長139.1メートルで幅員10.0メートル、左岸側は延長444.3メートルで幅員10.0メートルの取り付け道路を有している<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />。また、水道道路併用橋でもあり<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />橋に付帯設備として内径1.2メートルの水道管を4本（送水本管2本、工業用水道管2本）併設し、[[水路橋|水道橋]]の役割も持ちあわせている<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />。 また、災害時に防災拠点等に緊急輸送を行なうための、東京都の特定[[緊急輸送道路]]に指定されている<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|和書|date=2013 |url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/ |title=特定緊急輸送道路図 |publisher=東京都耐震ポータルサイト |accessdate=2017-03-30 |archiveurl= |archivedate= }}</ref>。 北岸は[[足立区]][[新田 (足立区)|新田]]（しんでん）2丁目、南岸は[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[神谷 (東京都北区)|神谷]]1丁目に位置する。橋名は北区の地名にちなむ。[[岩淵水門]]の管理橋を別とすれば、隅田川最上流の橋となる。

== 歴史 == もともと「[[隅田川の渡し|宮堀の渡し]]（神谷の渡しとも）」があったところである。現在の位置より100メートルほど南の[[王子神谷駅]]付近の[[神谷堀]]（甚兵衛堀とも呼ばれる）に[[昭和]]2年（[[1927年]]）に木鉄混交の橋として架けられた「神谷橋」があり、のち昭和25年（[[1950年]]）に鋼[[桁橋]]として改架された。新しく隅田川に架けることとなった当橋はこの名称にちなむ。昭和52年（[[1977年]]）に神谷堀が埋め立てられ[[廃橋]]となったことから、現在は神谷橋は現存しない。ちなみに、「神谷橋」の名は[[東京メトロ南北線]][[王子神谷駅]]の仮称としても使われていた。
橋は首都圏整備整備の一環として第一期工事が[[1962年]]（昭和37年）10月着工された。[[1965年]]（[[昭和]]40年）の創架当初は仮開通状態で、片側1車線の幅員10.0メートルであったが、[[1967年]]（昭和42年）2月<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm 新神谷橋1965-2-12] - 土木学会付属土木図書館、2017年2月24日閲覧。</ref>にはすぐに追加の拡幅工事が行われ、現在の幅員となった。橋桁制作は[[松尾橋梁]]<ref name="doboku1965" />下部工は坂田建設が行なった<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />。
== 路線バス == * [[都営バス]] ** [[都営バス千住営業所#王49系統|王49]] [[王子駅]] - 北区神谷町 - 江北陸橋下- [[大師前駅|西新井大師]] - [[梅島駅]] - [[都営バス千住営業所|千住車庫]] ** 王49 王子駅 - 北区神谷町 - 江北陸橋下 - 江北駅(平日朝のみ) ** 王49折返 王子駅 - 北区神谷町 - 江北陸橋下 - 西新井大師 - [[足立区役所]] * [[東武バスセントラル]] ** [[東武バスセントラル葛飾営業所#亀有駅北口 - （環七通り） - 足立区役所・王子駅線|王30]] 王子駅 - 北区神谷町 - 江北陸橋下 - 西新井大師 - [[綾瀬警察署]] - [[亀有駅]]北口（日中2往復のみ） ** 深夜31 王子駅 → 北区神谷町 → 江北陸橋下 → 西新井大師 → [[西新井駅]](平日深夜のみ) * [[国際興業バス]] ** [[国際興業バス赤羽営業所#赤羽駅東口 - ハートアイランド循環線|赤25]] [[赤羽駅]]東口 - 東十条四丁目 - ハートアイランド循環 **赤25-2 赤羽駅東口 → 東十条四丁目 → ハートアイランド西 → ハートアイランド東(夜間のみ) **赤25-3 ハートアイランド西 → ハートアイランド東 → 東十条四丁目 → 赤羽駅東口(早朝のみ) ** [[国際興業バス赤羽営業所#廃止・移管路線|赤26]] 赤羽駅東口 - 東十条四丁目 - 加賀団地 - 舎人団地 ** [[国際興業バス赤羽営業所#赤羽駅東口 - 江北陸橋（環七経由）- 西新井駅線|赤27]] 赤羽駅東口 - 東十条四丁目- 江北陸橋下 - 西新井大師 - [[西新井駅]] ** 赤27-2 [[国際興業バス赤羽営業所|赤羽車庫]] - 東十条四丁目 - 江北陸橋下 - 西新井大師 - 西新井駅 ** 赤27-3 赤羽駅東口 → 東十条四丁目 → 江北陸橋下(土曜夜間のみ)
== 隣の橋 == * [[隅田川]] : （上流） - [[岩淵水門]] - '''新神谷橋''' - [[新田橋 (東京都)|新田橋]] - [[新豊橋]] - （下流）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * {{Cite journal|和書|author=三上澄 |author2=佐藤次郎 |date=1965-07-01 |year=1965 |title=新神谷橋・鹿浜橋工事報告 |journal=[[土木技術 (雑誌)|土木技術]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=土木技術社 |issn=0285-5046 |ref=harv }}
== 関連項目 == * [[水道道路]] * [[鹿浜橋]] - 本橋のすぐ東側にある環状七号線の橋梁。本橋と同様に水道管を4本併設する。

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11503876]] Wikidata name: Shin Kamiya Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[:en:Shinden|Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[:en:Kamiya|Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[:en:Ōji Station|Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[:en:Umejima Station|Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[:en:Kameari Station|Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[:en:Nishiarai Station|Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[:en:Akabane Station|Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[:en:Ōji Station|Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[:en:Umejima Station|Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[:en:Kameari Station|Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[:en:Nishiarai Station|Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[:en:Akabane Station|Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11503876]] Wikidata name: Shin Kamiya Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[:en:Shinden|Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[:en:Kamiya|Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[:en:Ōji Station|Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[:en:Umejima Station|Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[:en:Kameari Station|Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[:en:Nishiarai Station|Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[:en:Akabane Station|Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[:en:Ōji Station|Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[:en:Umejima Station|Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[:en:Kameari Station|Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[:en:Nishiarai Station|Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[:en:Akabane Station|Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11503876]] Wikidata name: Shin Kamiya Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[:en:Shinden|Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[:en:Kamiya|Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[:en:Ōji Station|Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[:en:Umejima Station|Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[:en:Kameari Station|Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[:en:Nishiarai Station|Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[:en:Akabane Station|Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[:en:Ōji Station|Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[:en:Umejima Station|Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[:en:Kameari Station|Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[:en:Nishiarai Station|Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[:en:Akabane Station|Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

<!--Shinkamiya Bridge--><!--Shinkamiya Bridge--><!--Shinkamiya Bridge--><!--Shinkamiya Bridge--><!--Shinkamiya Bridge--><!--Shinkamiya Bridge--> [[Category:Road Facilities Opened in 1965]] [[Category:Aqueducts in Japan]] [[Category:Transport in Kita, Tokyo]] [[Category:Bridges in the special wards of Tokyo]] [[Category:Bridges in Adachi, Tokyo]] [[Category:Bridges over the Sumida River]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Shin Kamiya Bridge}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11503876}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11503876]] Wikidata name: Shin Kamiya Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[:en:Shinden|Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[:en:Kamiya|Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[:en:Ōji Station|Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[:en:Umejima Station|Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[:en:Kameari Station|Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[:en:Nishiarai Station|Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[:en:Akabane Station|Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.

(July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]] in [[Adachi Ward]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]]. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[:en:Shinden|Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[:en:Kamiya|Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>.
