== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in [[:en:Shinden|Shinden]] 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in [[:en:Kamiya|Kamiya]] 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: [[:en:Reinforced concrete|Reinforced concrete]] body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the [[:en:Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line]].
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * [[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]] ** [[Ō49]]: [[:en:Ōji Station|Ōji Station]] - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - [[:en:Umejima Station|Umejima Station]] - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - [[:en:Kameari Station|Kameari Station]] North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → [[:en:Nishiarai Station|Nishiarai Station]] * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: [[:en:Akabane Station|Akabane Station]] East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]]: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

== Bus Services == * {{ill|Toei Bus|en|Toei Bus|WD=Q859227|lt=Toei Bus}} ** [[Ō49]]: {{ill|Ōji Station|en|Ōji Station|WD=Q8081538|lt=Ōji Station}} - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - {{ill|Umejima Station|en|Umejima Station|WD=Q7881477|lt=Umejima Station}} - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - {{ill|Kameari Station|en|Kameari Station|WD=Q1952873|lt=Kameari Station}} North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → {{ill|Nishiarai Station|en|Nishiarai Station|WD=Q841838|lt=Nishiarai Station}} * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: {{ill|Akabane Station|en|Akabane Station|WD=Q800369|lt=Akabane Station}} East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11503876]] Wikidata name: Shin Kamiya Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:22, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River}} in [[Adachi Ward]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in {{ill|Shinden|en|Shinden|WD=Q2279041|lt=Shinden}} 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in {{ill|Kamiya|en|Kamiya|WD=Q90038560|lt=Kamiya}} 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: {{ill|Reinforced concrete|en|Reinforced concrete|WD=Q184190|lt=Reinforced concrete}} body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|en|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|WD=Q520772|lt=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line}}.
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * {{ill|Toei Bus|en|Toei Bus|WD=Q859227|lt=Toei Bus}} ** [[Ō49]]: {{ill|Ōji Station|en|Ōji Station|WD=Q8081538|lt=Ōji Station}} - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - {{ill|Umejima Station|en|Umejima Station|WD=Q7881477|lt=Umejima Station}} - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - {{ill|Kameari Station|en|Kameari Station|WD=Q1952873|lt=Kameari Station}} North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → {{ill|Nishiarai Station|en|Nishiarai Station|WD=Q841838|lt=Nishiarai Station}} * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: {{ill|Akabane Station|en|Akabane Station|WD=Q800369|lt=Akabane Station}} East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River}}: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Shin Kamiya Bridge]][[ja:新神谷橋]]
[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River}} in [[Adachi Ward]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in {{ill|Shinden|en|Shinden|WD=Q2279041|lt=Shinden}} 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in {{ill|Kamiya|en|Kamiya|WD=Q90038560|lt=Kamiya}} 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: {{ill|Reinforced concrete|en|Reinforced concrete|WD=Q184190|lt=Reinforced concrete}} body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|en|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|WD=Q520772|lt=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line}}.
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * {{ill|Toei Bus|en|Toei Bus|WD=Q859227|lt=Toei Bus}} ** [[Ō49]]: {{ill|Ōji Station|en|Ōji Station|WD=Q8081538|lt=Ōji Station}} - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - {{ill|Umejima Station|en|Umejima Station|WD=Q7881477|lt=Umejima Station}} - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - {{ill|Kameari Station|en|Kameari Station|WD=Q1952873|lt=Kameari Station}} North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → {{ill|Nishiarai Station|en|Nishiarai Station|WD=Q841838|lt=Nishiarai Station}} * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: {{ill|Akabane Station|en|Akabane Station|WD=Q800369|lt=Akabane Station}} East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River}}: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite journal|author=Mikami Sumi |author2=Sato Jiro |date=1965-07-01 |title=Shinkamiya Bridge and Shikahama Bridge Construction Report |journal=[[Civil Engineering Technology]] |volume=20 |issue=7 |pages=64-70 |publisher=Civil Engineering Technology Publishing |ref=harv }}
== Related Articles == * [[Waterworks road]] * [[Shikahama Bridge]] - Another Kanjō Nana-gō bridge with similar features.

=== Specifications === * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': ** Superstructure: Gerber (cantilever) plate girder/simply supported composite plate girder bridge ** Substructure: {{ill|Reinforced concrete|en|Reinforced concrete|WD=Q184190|lt=Reinforced concrete|1=Reinforced concrete}} body/steel pipe pile foundation (600 mm outer diameter, 14.0–16.5 m length)<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" /> * '''Total length''': 153.0 m<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87">{{Cite web|date=1965-03|url=http://library.jsce.or.jp/jsce/open/00034/50-03/50-3-009.pdf|title=Journal of Civil Engineering, Volume 50, Issue 3|publisher=Japan Society of Civil Engineers|format=PDF|pages=87-88|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref> * '''Overall width''': 21.0 m<ref name="dobokuTec6507-66">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=66-67}}</ref> * '''Effective width''': 20.0 m (roadway: 8.0 m, sidewalks: 2.0 m) * '''Span configuration''': 46.0 m + 61.0 m + 46.0 m * '''Opening date''': February 12, 1965 (Showa 40)<ref name="gakkaishi6403-87" /> * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government
== History == Originally, the ''Miyabori Ferry'' (also called the ''Kamiya Ferry'') operated in this area. In 1927 (Showa 2), the ''Kamiya Bridge,'' a mixed wood-and-steel structure, was constructed approximately 100 meters south of the current location near [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] and replaced by a steel girder bridge in 1950 (Showa 25). The current bridge’s name derives from this earlier structure. The original Kamiya Bridge was abandoned in 1977 (Showa 52) when the Kamiya Canal (also known as Jinbei Canal) was filled in. The name "Kamiya Bridge" was also a proposed name for [[:en:Ōji-Kamiya Station|Ōji-Kamiya Station]] on the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|en|Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|WD=Q520772|lt=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line|1=Tokyo Metro Namboku Line}}.
The first phase of the Shinkamiya Bridge construction began in October 1962 (Showa 37) as part of regional infrastructure improvements. Initially, the bridge opened in 1965 (Showa 40) with a temporary two-lane roadway. It was expanded to its current width in February 1967 (Showa 42)<ref name="doboku1965">[http://library.jsce.or.jp/jscelib/h_bridge/46076.htm Shinkamiya Bridge, February 12, 1965] - Japan Society of Civil Engineers Library, accessed January 2, 2025.</ref>. The bridge girders were manufactured by [[Matsuo Bridge Company]], and the substructure was constructed by Sakata Construction Company<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />.
== Bus Services == * {{ill|Toei Bus|en|Toei Bus|WD=Q859227|lt=Toei Bus|1=Toei Bus}} ** [[Ō49]]: {{ill|Ōji Station|en|Ōji Station|WD=Q8081538|lt=Ōji Station|1=Ōji Station}} - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - [[Nishiarai Daishi]] - {{ill|Umejima Station|en|Umejima Station|WD=Q7881477|lt=Umejima Station|1=Umejima Station}} - [[Senju Depot]] ** Variants: Ō49 (shortened routes to Kōhoku Overpass or Nishiarai Daishi) * [[Tobu Bus Central]] ** [[Ō30]]: Ōji Station - Kamiya-cho, Kita Ward - Kōhoku Overpass - Nishiarai Daishi - {{ill|Kameari Station|en|Kameari Station|WD=Q1952873|lt=Kameari Station|1=Kameari Station}} North Exit ** Deep night service: Ōji Station → Kamiya-cho → Nishiarai Daishi → {{ill|Nishiarai Station|en|Nishiarai Station|WD=Q841838|lt=Nishiarai Station|1=Nishiarai Station}} * [[Kokusai Kōgyō Bus]] ** [[Akabane 25]]: {{ill|Akabane Station|en|Akabane Station|WD=Q800369|lt=Akabane Station|1=Akabane Station}} East Exit - Tōjō 4-chome - Heart Island Loop ** Variants: Akabane 25-2 (to Heart Island West) and Akabane 25-3 (from Heart Island West)
== Adjacent Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River|1=Sumida River}}: ** (Upstream) - [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]] * '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' - [[Shinden Bridge (Tokyo)|Shinden Bridge]] - [[Shintoyohashi Bridge]] - (Downstream)

[[Image:Shin-Kamiyahashi.jpg|thumb|300px|right|Taken in Adachi Ward, Tokyo. (July 2008)]] {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=318|align=left}} '''Shinkamiya Bridge''' (新神谷橋) is a bridge over the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River|1=Sumida River}} in [[Adachi Ward]], {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}. It is part of the [[Tokyo Metropolitan Route 318]], also known as the ''Kanjō Nana-gō Line'' (環七通り), a [[city planning road]] serving both transportation and waterworks.
== Overview == The bridge spans 153.0 meters and is a three-span steel plate girder bridge designed as a class 1 bridge (TL-20). It includes approach roads of 139.1 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the right bank and 444.3 meters (10.0 meters wide) on the left bank<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64">{{harvtxt|Mikami Sumi|Sato Jiro|1965|pp=64-65}}</ref>. The bridge also functions as a [[waterworks road bridge]], accommodating four water pipes (two main water supply pipes and two industrial water pipes) with an inner diameter of 1.2 meters each<ref name="dobokuTec6507-64" />. It is designated as one of Tokyo’s critical [[emergency transportation roads]] for disaster response and relief efforts<ref name="taishinYuso">{{Cite web|date=2013|url=http://www.taishin.metro.tokyo.jp/yuso/|title=Special Emergency Transportation Road Map|publisher=Tokyo Earthquake Resistance Portal|accessdate=2025-01-02}}</ref>.
The northern end is located in {{ill|Shinden|en|Shinden|WD=Q2279041|lt=Shinden|1=Shinden}} 2-chome, Adachi Ward, and the southern end is in {{ill|Kamiya|en|Kamiya|WD=Q90038560|lt=Kamiya|1=Kamiya}} 1-chome, [[:en:Kita Ward|Kita Ward]], Tokyo. The name originates from the district of Kamiya in Kita Ward. Excluding the management bridge of the [[Iwabuchi Floodgate]], Shinkamiya Bridge is the northernmost bridge on the Sumida River.
